title: An Isoperimetric Inequality for the Hamming Cube and Integrality Gaps in Bounded-Degree Graphs
speaker: Siavosh Benabbas
abstract: In 1970s Paul Erdos asked the following question: Consider all the boolean strings of length n. Assume that one has chosen a subset S of the strings such that no two chosen strings are different in precisely n/4 (or its closest even integer) coordinates. How big can the set of chosen strings be? Erdos conjectured that the answer is small (in a precise sense) and put a $250 prize for a solution.

proof_stages.label: Erdős--Frankl--Rödl problem and density target
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the exact-distance graph on the Hamming cube, recalls the Frankl--Rödl theorem that large sets cannot avoid one prescribed distance, and formulates the new density theorem lower-bounding the fraction of exact-distance pairs inside dense sets, including a two-family d-or-d+1 version.

proof_stages.label: Reduction target via product noise
proof_stages.summary: After explaining why the original Frankl--Rödl reduction to shared-one counts loses too much density, the proof instead compares the exact-distance pair distribution with the product noise distribution of expected distance d, where known noise-stability lower bounds apply.

proof_stages.label: Fourier-operator comparison
proof_stages.summary: The two probabilities are written as quadratic forms in the indicator function using the Bonami--Beckner noise operator and the exact-sphere averaging operator, then compared spectrally through their common Fourier-character eigenbasis and Krawtchouk eigenvalues, with d and d+1 averaged to handle parity.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative transfer and one-family parity extraction
proof_stages.summary: The spectral closeness gives an o(1) additive transfer from the product-noise lower bound to the exact-distance model, and the one-family theorem removes the d+1 distance by passing to the denser parity class of the set.

proof_stages.label: Relaxations, integrality gaps, and hierarchy framework
proof_stages.summary: For the application, the talk recalls bounded-degree vertex cover and independent set approximation, defines LP/SDP relaxations and integrality gaps, and explains how Sherali--Adams and Lovász--Schrijver lift-and-project hierarchies strengthen basic formulations.

proof_stages.label: Sampling criterion for bounded-degree gaps
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a general-graph integrality-gap instance, the argument samples edges to obtain bounded average and then maximum degree; the relaxation value only decreases, so the remaining task is to prove that every large complement still contains many sampled edges.

proof_stages.label: Hamming-cube density theorem yields bounded-degree hierarchy gaps
proof_stages.summary: Because the relevant gap constructions use the Frankl--Rödl exact-distance graph on the Hamming cube, the new density theorem supplies the edge-density condition needed after sampling, giving Lovász--Schrijver SDP gaps for vertex cover and Sherali--Adams LP gaps for independent set in maximum-degree d graphs.

examples.name: Even-parity subset obstruction
examples.note: The all-even Hamming-weight subset is used as a parity obstruction showing why a two-family theorem cannot generally control only one distance and must involve two distances of opposite parity.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the even-parity set to explain the parity obstruction and later runs the parity-splitting trick to eliminate the d+1 distance in the one-family case

examples.name: All-one-half fractional vertex-cover solution
examples.note: The assignment x_i=1/2 for every vertex illustrates why the basic LP relaxation can have value at most n/2 and hence a large integrality gap.
examples.engagement_reason: he verifies the all-1/2 fractional assignment is always feasible and computes its objective as at most n/2 to illustrate the LP gap
